content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence of integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ and an integer KKK. Find the number of contiguous subsequences AL,AL+1,…,ARA_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_RAL​,AL+1​,…,AR​ such that R−L+1≥KR-L+1 \ge KR−L+1≥K and the KKK-th element of the subsequence (AL+K−1A_{L+K-1}AL+K−1​) is equal to the maximum of all elements of the entire sequence.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer --- the number of contiguous subsequences satisfying the given conditions.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the number of contiguous subarrays whose length is at least K and whose K‑th element equals the maximum element of the whole array.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The global maximum is 5 at position 5 (1‑based). Since K=3, the subarray must start at L=5‑3+1=3. The end R can be any index from 5 to 5, giving exactly one valid subarray (3,4,5).

Maximum value 5 occurs at positions 2,3,5. For each:
- i=2 → L=1, R can be 2..6 → 5 subarrays.
- i=3 → L=2, R can be 3..6 → 4 subarrays.
- i=5 → L=4, R can be 5..6 → 2 subarrays.
Total = 5+4+2 = 11.
Maximum 3 at positions 1 and 3. With K=1 the subarray starts at those positions. From position 1 we have 4 possible ends, from position 3 we have 2 possible ends. 4+2=6.

solution.approach: For each test case find the global maximum M of the array. Every occurrence of M at position i (1‑based) can serve as the K‑th element of a subarray only if i ≥ K, because the subarray must start at L = i‑K+1 ≥ 1. For such an i the start L is fixed, while the end R can be any index from i to N. Hence there are (N‑i+1) possible subarrays for this i. Summing (N‑i+1) over all positions i where Ai = M and i ≥ K gives the answer. The algorithm scans the array once to find M, then scans again to accumulate the sum, yielding O(N) time and O(1) extra space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(∑N) over the whole input.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it); K = next(it)
        arr = [next(it) for _ in range(N)]
        mx = max(arr)
        ans = 0
        for idx, val in enumerate(arr):
            if val == mx and idx + 1 >= K:
                ans += N - idx
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
